androidVersions.title: Android 1.0 – 1.1 (No Codename)
androidVersions.whatsNew:
First Android OS release
Google apps (Gmail, Maps, YouTube)
Android Market introduced
androidVersions.explanation: Google released its first commercial Android version 1.0 in 2008. This version of Android was very basic but had Google apps like Gmail, YouTube, Calendar, Maps, Search, Instant Messaging, and many more. All of these applications were integrated directly into the Android OS. It also supported HTML and XHTML web pages, featured a camera, Wi-Fi, and included Bluetooth support.

androidVersions.title: Android 1.5 – Cupcake
androidVersions.whatsNew:
On-screen keyboard
Widgets support
Video recording
androidVersions.explanation: In 2009, Android released its second major release, Android version 1.5 Cupcake. With this release, the tradition of naming Android versions after confectionery started. With Cupcake, Android introduced the first on-screen keyboard as people moved to touchscreen smartphones from keypad-style handsets. They also introduced a framework for third-party app widgets, which was a significant step. Cupcake also introduced the platform’s first-ever video recording option.

androidVersions.title: Android 1.6 – Donut
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Improved search functionality
Support for multiple screen sizes
Enhanced camera features
androidVersions.explanation: In the fall of 2009, Android dropped its next major update, Android 1.6 Donut. It came out at the right time, as the world slowly transitioned towards bigger screens and resolution phones. Donut came with built-in support for CDMA networks, which helped Android grow quickly. In addition, it introduced multiple new features like voice and text entry search, bookmark history, and WVGA resolution. It also allowed the users to select multiple photos for deletion at a time.

androidVersions.title: Android 2.0 – 2.1 – Eclair
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Google Maps Navigation
Multiple account support
Improved UI and performance
androidVersions.explanation: Android 2.0 Eclair was released just six weeks after Donut. Android, with this version, gained widespread popularity among the masses due to the hype surrounding Motorola’s DROID phone and the marketing campaign led by Verizon. It introduced several key features, including SMS, MMS, voice-guided turn-by-turn navigation, real-time traffic information, pinch-to-zoom capability, Bluetooth 2.1, and several bug fixes.

androidVersions.title: Android 2.2 – Froyo
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Faster performance (JIT compiler)
Wi-Fi hotspot support
Improved browser speed
androidVersions.explanation: Froyo was released four months after Eclair’s introduction. This version of Android primarily focused on enhancing back-end performance, improving speed, and optimizing memory usage. In addition, it introduced voice actions, which allowed users to perform basic functions like speaking a command, making notes, and getting directions. It also supported Adobe Flash.

androidVersions.title: Android 2.3 – Gingerbread
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Improved UI (dark theme)
Better gaming performance
Enhanced battery management
androidVersions.explanation: With Gingerbread, Android began to adopt a distinctive visual design. For example, the Android mascot is green in color, and this version’s prominent colors were Green and Black. In addition, Gingerbread supported an extra-large screen, a simplified interface, enhanced copy/paste functions, NFC (Near Field Communication), and a host of improvements.

androidVersions.title: Android 3.0 – 3.2 – Honeycomb
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Tablet-optimized UI
System bar and action bar
Improved multitasking
androidVersions.explanation: Android released the Honeycomb version in 2011 for the first Android-based tablet, the Motorola Xoom. With subsequent updates, 3.1 and 3.2, the Honeycomb remained a tablet-exclusive entity. This was a detour for Android’s visual appeal, as this time it had a colored holographic design. It was designed to make the most of the tablet’s widescreen space.

androidVersions.title: Android 4.0 – Ice Cream Sandwich
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Unified phone and tablet UI
Face Unlock
Improved multitasking
androidVersions.explanation: Ice Cream Sandwich marked the entry of Android into the modern design language. It refined all the visual elements with a single, unified UI vision that reunited phone and tablet design. It carried over the card-like appearance from Honeycomb and also introduced swiping. It also introduced a framework for design standardization, known as ‘Holo’.

androidVersions.title: Android 4.1 – 4.3 – Jelly Bean
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Project Butter (smooth UI)
Google Now
Improved notifications
androidVersions.explanation: Android Jelly Bean was introduced in 2012 and made the best impressions among new users. Jelly Bean was built on the foundation built by Android version 4.0. It polished many rough edges, making the OS more attractive and appealing. In addition, it improved accessibility and offered multiple features like screen lock, bug removal, 4K support, and Google Now.

androidVersions.title: Android 4.4 – KitKat
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Optimized for low-end devices
Immersive mode
Voice search (“OK Google”)
androidVersions.explanation: Late in 2013, Android released KitKat. This Android version introduced several key features, including ‘OK Google’ support, offline music support, smart caller ID, and improved application compatibility, among others.

androidVersions.title: Android 5.0 – 5.1 – Lollipop
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Material Design
New animations
Improved notifications
androidVersions.explanation: With the version Lollipop, Android reinvented itself. Amongst all these versions in the Android list, it established the material design standard, which stands even today. It gave the OS a fresh and new visual look. It also introduced at-a-glance access for all the notifications from the lock screen itself and improved ‘OK Google’ support.

androidVersions.title: Android 6.0 – Marshmallow
androidVersions.whatsNew:
App permissions control
Fingerprint unlock
Doze battery saving
androidVersions.explanation: With Marshmallow, Android started the trend of releasing a significant update per year. It introduced support for fingerprint readers, USB-C, the App Standby feature, and Doze mode to save battery life, among other notable features.

androidVersions.title: Android 7.0 – 7.1 – Nougat
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Split-screen multitasking
Improved notifications
Better performance
androidVersions.explanation: Android Nougat is popular for releasing the Google Assistant. It offered features including split-screen mode, a Data Saver feature, file-based encryption, battery usage alerts, and a zoom-in screen.

androidVersions.title: Android 8.0 – 8.1 – Oreo
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Picture-in-picture mode
Faster boot time
Notification dots
androidVersions.explanation: Oreo introduced several notable features, including picture-in-picture support, adaptive icons, 2x booting speed, Google Play Protect, a notification snoozing option, and many other features. It also contributed to Project Treble.

androidVersions.title: Android 9 – Pie
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Gesture navigation
AI battery optimization
Digital Wellbeing
androidVersions.explanation: Pie brought some fresh air to the Android mobile OS. The most popular change was the hybrid gesture/button navigation system. Pie also introduced many productivity features, security and privacy enhancements, as well as intelligent systems to manage power and screen brightness.

androidVersions.title: Android 10
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Dark mode
Privacy controls
Gesture navigation
androidVersions.explanation: With this version, Android dropped the naming tradition of each major release along with an item of confectionery. This version introduced even more visual interface upgrades for Android gestures and a swipe-driven navigation approach along with granular permissions and control over location data.

androidVersions.title: Android 11
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Chat bubbles
Screen recording
Media controls
androidVersions.explanation: Android 11 was released during the COVID-19 pandemic. It focused on privacy with more granular app permissions, simplified panels, conversation-style notifications, native screen-recording features, and connected-device controls.

androidVersions.title: Android 12
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Material You
Dynamic colors
Privacy dashboard
androidVersions.explanation: Android 12 completely transformed the standard to create Material You, a very visually customizable Android version. It renewed its focus on widgets and easy accessibility.

androidVersions.title: Android 13
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Per-app language
Better privacy
Improved media controls
androidVersions.explanation: Android 13 is a stable OS launched in 2022 with a new interface design. It made strides in improving security, privacy, and performance while laying the foundation for bigger screens and foldables.

androidVersions.title: Android 14
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Battery improvements
Accessibility features
Larger font scaling
androidVersions.explanation: Android 14 aims to provide rich camera and media experiences with Ultra HDR and lossless USB audio. It offers per-app languages, predictive text, Health Connect, and Credential Manager.

androidVersions.title: Android 15
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Multitasking improvements
Privacy updates
Satellite connectivity
androidVersions.explanation: This version was set to launch in the fourth quarter of 2024 with several new features and enhanced capabilities including improved multitasking, privacy, and satellite connectivity.

androidVersions.title: Android 16
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Advanced AI Integration
On-device AI Processing
Smarter Battery Optimization
Improved Multitasking UI
Stronger Privacy & Security
androidVersions.explanation: Android 16 focuses on enhancing multitasking, accessibility, user privacy, performance, and device compatibility. Key features include Live Activities, improved support for larger screens and foldables, and advanced camera capabilities.

androidVersions.title: Android 17
androidVersions.whatsNew:
Enhanced AI integration
Better foldable device support
Improved battery optimization
androidVersions.explanation: As of October 2025, Android 17 is the upcoming Android release, anticipated for mid-2026. It is expected to focus on enhancing AI integration, foldable device support, and battery optimization.
